summary: I was born and raised in Somalia, where I trained to be an air traffic controller. Back then, Somalia was peaceful, especially in my area, before the civil war broke out. I lived through the turmoil and witnessed firsthand the impact of the conflict on my people.

entity: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
summary: In Mogadishu, I often saw the UNHCR office with a glass window displaying a slogan that said, 'Everybody can be a refugee. It only takes natural disaster or man-made disaster.' Those words made me reflect deeply, especially as the Somali Civil War unfolded and many people, including seniors, were forced to flee.

The Somali Civil War was a terrible man-made disaster that changed everything. I was in Mogadishu when it happened, and I saw many people forced to flee, including seniors who could not walk and had to be carried by their children. This event marked a turning point in my life and led me to eventually leave Somalia.

I lived in Mogadishu, the capital of Somalia, during the peaceful times and through the outbreak of the civil war. It was there I saw the UNHCR office and witnessed the devastating effects of the conflict on the community. Mogadishu holds many memories of both hope and hardship for me.

I emigrated from Somalia to the United States in 1993 seeking a new life after the civil war. I first lived in Washington, D.C. for two years before moving to Minnesota. The U.S. has been the place where I built my career, ran for office, and became involved in community advocacy.

After arriving in the United States, I lived in Washington, D.C. for two years before relocating to Minnesota. Those early years in D.C. were a transition period as I adjusted to life in America and planned my next steps. It was a foundational time before settling in the Midwest.

I moved to Minnesota in the summer of 1995 and started working in the mailroom before becoming a bilingual school-community liaison for Minneapolis Public Schools. Minnesota became home where I organized Somali education events and ran for mayor of Minneapolis in 2001. I also worked for Senator Norm Coleman and later joined CommonBond Communities to support affordable housing.

In Minneapolis, I worked as a bilingual school-community liaison and organized annual Somali education nights to support students and families. I ran for mayor of Minneapolis in 2001, becoming the first Somali American to run for public office in the city and the U.S. Although I did not win, it marked my emergence as a community leader.

The day of the Minneapolis mayoral primary in 2001 coincided with the September 11 attacks in New York. The tragedy overshadowed the election day and deeply affected the community. Despite losing the election, that day marked a significant moment in my life and in the nation's history.

I worked on the staff of former Minnesota Senator Norm Coleman for six and a half years, focusing on immigration and other cases. Senator Coleman was an important figure in my political career, and I supported him through his reelection campaign and the lengthy recount process. After he lost, I moved to Faribault but stayed engaged in community work.
cues: worked six and a half years

summary: After Senator Norm Coleman lost his reelection, I moved to Faribault, a small town in Minnesota, where I ran a small convenience grocery business. The business did not work out, so I sold it and returned to the Twin Cities to continue my work in affordable housing and community advocacy.

I joined CommonBond Communities, the largest affordable housing developer in Minnesota, Iowa, and Wisconsin, because I developed a strong interest in affordable housing. I currently work there as an advantage service specialist, connecting residents with resources and volunteers to help them reach their goals. This role allows me to continue serving the community in a meaningful way.
